text:
The direct conversion of methane to higher value chemicals remains one of the most significant challenges in catalysis. Currently, acetic acid, one of the most important industrial chemicals, is produced on a multi-billion dollar scale via the carbonylation of methanol using expensive and highly unsustainable homogeneous catalysts and stoichiometric reagents. Herein, we report the production of acetic acid from methane and carbon monoxide under solar-driven photocatalytic conditions at ambient temperature using only water as the external oxygen source.

Our improved photocatalytic system consists of a TiO2 material functionalized with ammonium phosphotungstic clusters of nanometric size loaded with isolated Pt single atoms (Pt1). With this system we are able to stably produce in solution up to 5.7 mmol/L of acetic acid for at least 60 h and, using all data obtained, selectivity of over 90% is achieved in the liquid phase (solution and reaction vial) and 66% on the carbon balance, giving 99 acetic acid moles for each Pt mole. A detailed mechanistic study revealed that acetic acid is synthesized via photocatalytic oxidative carbonylation of methane at Pt1, and it is aided by the strong catalytic character of the isolated Pt atoms. The high reactivity of the C-H bond in methane can be stimulated by the hydroxyl radicals formed under light illumination from the water solution and so enabling catalysis in a very sustainable way.
explanation: The passage is a highly polished, technique-heavy abstract with precise numerical metrics that is often seen in AI-generated text; however, the extraordinary claim could also come from genuine research.
